frameworks.name: Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: Perceptual decisions modeled as weighted combination of prior and likelihood.

frameworks.name: heuristic combination
frameworks.one_line: Simpler non-Bayesian rule invoked when priors are explicitly communicated.

core_question: Do humans use implicit (learned) and explicit (communicated) priors in the same way during perceptual inference?
key_assumptions: Perception integrates prior knowledge with sensory likelihood
Priors can be acquired implicitly through observation or delivered explicitly
Bayesian modeling can quantify the weights given to prior and likelihood
manipulations: Source of the prior: implicit (learned from statistics) vs explicit (communicated)
Tasks designed to probe prior weighting under matched sensory likelihoods
key_findings: Sensory likelihood weights were highly correlated across implicit and explicit tasks, slightly stronger for implicit
Prior weights were poorly correlated across tasks and markedly smaller when priors were explicit
Model comparison favored different computations for implicit vs explicit priors
interpretations: Implicit priors are integrated more closely to Bayesian optimality, whereas explicit priors are handled by a simpler heuristic
This dissociation may reconcile prior conflicting reports on whether humans use priors appropriately in perception
